questions.scenario: A script uses a CONFIG_DIR variable that might not be set. Use a safe default if it's unset.
questions.question: Print ${CONFIG_DIR:-/etc/reactor} — use the default /etc/reactor if CONFIG_DIR is unset.
questions.description: ${var:-default} returns the variable's value if set and non-empty, or the default otherwise. The variable is NOT assigned — it's just a safe read. Use ${var:=default} if you want assignment.
use_cases: Safe defaults for optional env vars
hints: CONFIG_DIR is not set, so the default fires

questions.scenario: A required variable SITE_ID must be set before the script can continue. If it's not set, the script must abort with a message.
questions.question: Use ${SITE_ID:?} to print an error and exit if SITE_ID is not set.
questions.description: ${var:?message} aborts the script with an error if var is unset or empty. This is the standard way to enforce required variables at the top of scripts — much better than checking manually.
use_cases: Validate input variables before destructive operations
hints: SITE_ID is not set so it will error
Run: echo ${SITE_ID:?'SITE_ID is required'} and observe the error

questions.scenario: You need to know how many characters are in the HOSTNAME variable.
questions.question: Print the length of $HOSTNAME using parameter expansion.
questions.description: ${#var} returns the number of characters in the variable's value. Works for strings and arrays (where it returns the number of elements). No external command needed.
use_cases: Validate that a variable isn't too long
Pad or trim strings based on length
Check that a required field isn't empty without using -z

questions.scenario: A log file has the path /home/student/logs/app.log. You need just the filename without the directory.
questions.question: Use parameter expansion to strip the directory prefix from '/home/student/logs/app.log'.
questions.description: ${var##prefix} removes the longest matching prefix pattern. ${var#prefix} removes the shortest. ## greedy, # non-greedy. Use */ to strip everything up to and including the last slash — equivalent to basename.
use_cases: Extract filename from a path without calling basename
Remove a fixed prefix from variable values in a loop
hints: ${var##*/} removes everything up to the last /

questions.scenario: A config value stored as 'status_offline' needs to be normalised to uppercase.
questions.question: Convert the variable STATUS='status_offline' to uppercase using parameter expansion.
questions.description: ${var^^} converts to uppercase (bash 4+). ${var,,} converts to lowercase. ${var^} capitalises only the first character. No external commands (tr, awk) needed for simple case conversion.
use_cases: Ensure consistent casing in log messages

questions.scenario: A message template contains the word 'ERROR' that needs to be replaced with 'ALERT' before logging.
questions.question: Replace all occurrences of 'ERROR' with 'ALERT' in MSG='ERROR: disk ERROR detected'.
questions.description: ${var//pattern/replacement} replaces ALL occurrences of pattern. ${var/pattern/replacement} replaces only the first. No sed needed for simple string substitution within a variable.
use_cases: Sanitise variable values without external commands
hints: Run: MSG='ERROR: disk ERROR detected' && echo ${MSG//ERROR/ALERT}
